title: Predicting Noise at Each Step
concept: Rather than predicting the clean image directly, the network is trained to predict exactly what noise was added at each specific timestep.

exercises.prompt: When you look at a blurry, static-filled TV screen, what would help you most to fix it?

options.text: Knowing exactly what static was added to the picture
Guessing a brand new random picture

exercises.explain: If you know exactly what noise/static got added, you can subtract it and reveal the clean picture underneath!

cards.title: Guess the Noise, Not the Picture!
cards.body: Here's a surprising trick AI image generators use: instead of trying to guess the whole clean picture in one go, the network just tries to guess the tiny bit of noise that was sprinkled on at that exact step. Guess the noise, remove it, and the picture gets clearer!

cards.title: What Does This Mean?
cards.body: During training, a clean image has noise added to it at many steps. Instead of asking the network 'what did the original image look like?', we ask it 'what noise did I just add at THIS step?' The network learns to spot and predict that specific noise pattern, which is an easier, more focused job.

cards.title: Finding the Smudge, Not Redrawing the Page
cards.body: Imagine someone smudged a tiny bit of eraser dust onto your homework page. It's way easier to spot and brush away just the dust than to rewrite the whole page from scratch. Predicting noise is like spotting the smudge — small, specific, and easy to remove!

cards.title: Step-by-Step Cleanup
cards.body: At step 500 of 1000, a photo of a cat is covered in a certain amount of random static. The network looks at this noisy cat and predicts: 'I think THIS specific static pattern was added.' It subtracts that predicted noise, and the image becomes a little clearer — closer to the real cat photo.

cards.title: Myth: The AI Draws the Final Image Directly
cards.body: Many people think the network just imagines the final clean picture right away. In truth, it never directly draws the final image in one shot — it only predicts small noise patterns over and over, and the clean image slowly emerges as those predictions get subtracted step by step.

cards.title: Two Ways to Train the Network
cards.body: Compare two possible training goals for the same noisy image.

left.text: Network must imagine the ENTIRE original picture at once — a huge, hard task with lots of missing details.

right.text: Network only needs to spot the specific static pattern added — a smaller, more consistent, easier task.

exercises.prompt: What does the network actually predict at each training step?

options.text: The exact noise added at that timestep
The name of the object in the image
The number of pixels in the image

exercises.explain: The network's job is to predict the specific noise pattern added at that exact step, not the whole final picture.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Predicting the noise at each step is generally an easier task than predicting the entire clean image at once.
exercises.explain: True! Spotting a small, specific noise pattern is simpler and more consistent than imagining a whole complex image from scratch.

exercises.sentence: Instead of predicting the clean image, the network predicts the ___ added at each step.
exercises.explain: The network is trained to predict exactly what noise was added at each specific timestep, then that noise is subtracted to reveal a clearer image.

exercises.prompt: Why is predicting noise better than predicting the whole clean image directly?

options.text: Because the noise pattern is a smaller, more consistent target that's easier to learn
Because noise is prettier to look at than clean images
Because computers can't see clean images at all

exercises.explain: Noise is a simpler, more predictable target at each step, which makes training more stable and effective than trying to guess the full image.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'The AI model directly paints the final clear picture from nothing in one single step.' What's wrong with this statement?

options.text: Nothing — that's exactly how it works
The model actually predicts noise at each step and removes it gradually, not the clean image all at once
The model only works with black and white images
The model paints using real paint and brushes

exercises.explain: The model never directly paints the final image in one shot. Instead, it predicts the noise added at each timestep and subtracts it, gradually revealing a clear image over many small steps.
